content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To     : Jeanne                                    Date  :  9-29-76

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Dawn

Subject: Advice Letter from Edward Heap, Candidate for Barstow Municipal
        Court

Since Mr. Heap asked for immediate advice regarding the
necessity for filing a Statement of Economic Interests
I called him on this date.

My advice was that, if he filed the declaration of candidacy
prior to May 5, 1976, the effective date of the bill that
required judges of courts of records to file statements, he
did not have to file a statement. He said he did not remember when he filed his declaration of candidacy but that it
was at the proper time for filing prior to the primary election
in June. This period was from Feb. 14 (approx.) to March 12
(approx.). Based on this assumption, I advised that he
did not have to file a statement of Economic Interests as
a candidate for office. I also advised that if he is elected
to office, he will have to file a form 720 within 30 days
after assuming office.

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or contains only very faint marks that are not legible enough to transcribe with confidence. There are some barely visible curved marks near the top of the page, but they are too faint to read with certainty.

EDWARD L. HEAP
ATTORNEY AT LAW
325 EAST MAIN STREET
BARSTOW CALIFORNIA 92311

Telephone
Area Code 714
256-6823

September 23, 1976


State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

Please be advised I am a candidate for the Barstow
Municipal Court which sets in Barstow, California. Just recently
I was reading where Government Code Section 87200 was amended
to include judges of courts of record. I assume from this amend-
ment I am required to file with your office a Form 720. I would
appreciate your immediate advice on this matter. I have never
received notification from any one as to necessity for filing, and
I obviously wish to comply with the law.

Yours very truly,

Edward L. Heap

ELH/rm

[The image appears to be almost entirely blank or too faint to read reliably. There are some very faint markings visible at the top and scattered throughout the page, but they are too unclear to transcribe accurately. Without being able to make out the text with confidence, I cannot provide a reliable transcription that follows the instruction to copy text verbatim.]
